title: Self-Soothing A way to remember these skills is to think of soothing each of your five senses. With Vision: 

334
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 8 (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 6â€“6b)
Self-Soothing
A way to remember these skills is to think of soothing each of your five senses.
With Vision:

Â‰ Look at the stars at night.

Â‰ Look at pictures you like in a book.

Â‰ Buy one beautiful flower.

Â‰ Make one space in a room pleasing to look at.

Â‰ Light a candle and watch the flame.

Â‰ Set a pretty place at the table using your best
things.

Â‰ Go people-watching or window-shopping.

Â‰ Go to a museum or poster shop with beautiful
art.

Â‰ Sit in the lobby of a beautiful old hotel.

Â‰ Look at nature around you.

Â‰ Walk in a pretty part of town.

Â‰ Watch a sunrise or a sunset.

Â‰ Go to a dance performance, or watch it on TV.

Â‰ Be mindful of each sight that passes in front of
you.

Â‰ Take a walk in a park or a scenic hike.

Â‰ Browse through stores looking at things.

Â‰ Other:
With Hearing:

Â‰ Listen to soothing or invigorating music.

Â‰ Pay attention to sounds of nature (waves,
birds, rainfall, leaves rustling).

Â‰ Pay attention to the sounds of the city (traffic,
horns, city music).

Â‰ Sing to your favorite songs.

Â‰ Hum a soothing tune.

Â‰ Learn to play an instrument.

Â‰ Burn a CD or make an iPod mix with music
that will get you through tough times. Turn it
on.

Â‰ Be mindful of any sounds that come your way,
letting them go in one ear and out the other.

Â‰ Turn on the radio.

Â‰ Other:
With Smell:

Â‰ Use your favorite soap, shampoo, aftershave,
cologne, or lotions, or try them on in the store.

Â‰ Burn incense or light a scented candle.

Â‰ Open a package of coffee and inhale the
aroma.

Â‰ Put lemon oil on your furniture.

Â‰ Put potpourri or eucalyptus oil in a bowl in your
room.

Â‰ Sit in a new car and breathe the aroma.

Â‰ Boil cinnamon. Make cookies, bread, or
popcorn.

Â‰ Smell the roses.

Â‰ Walk in a wooded area and mindfully breathe
in the fresh smells of nature.

Â‰ Open the window and smell the air.

Â‰ Other:
With Taste:

Â‰ Eat some of your favorite foods.

Â‰ Drink your favorite soothing drink, such
as herbal tea, hot chocolate, a lattĂ©, or a
smoothie.

Â‰ Treat yourself to a dessert.

Â‰ Eat macaroni and cheese or another favorite
childhood food.

Â‰ Sample flavors in an ice cream store.

Â‰ Suck on a piece of peppermint candy.

Â‰ Chew your favorite gum.

Â‰ Get a little bit of a special food you donâ€™t
usually spend the money on, such as fresh-
squeezed orange juice or your favorite candy.

Â‰ Really taste the food you eat. Eat one thing
mindfully.

Â‰ Other:
With Touch:

Â‰ Take a long hot bath or shower.

Â‰ Pet your dog or cat.

Â‰ Have a massage. Soak your feet.

Â‰ Put creamy lotion on your whole body.

Â‰ Put a cold compress on your forehead.

Â‰ Sink into a comfortable chair in your home.

Â‰ Put on a blouse or shirt that has a pleasant
feel.

Â‰ Take a drive with the car windows rolled down.

Â‰ Run your hand along smooth wood or leather.

Â‰ Hug someone.

Â‰ Put clean sheets on the bed.

Â‰ Wrap up in a blanket.

Â‰ Notice touch that is soothing.

Â‰ Other:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Self-Soothing A way to remember these skills is to think of soothing each of your five senses. With Vision: 

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.routine_role: monthly review or larger pattern tool
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: not auto-loaded on survival days; use as a scheduled monthly review item
routine_integration.recommended_block: monthly support audit or household reset
